subject: Chemistry
chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes; Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

questions.question: The IUPAC name of CH3CH2Cl is ______.
questions.explanation: CH3CH2Cl contains a two-carbon ethane chain with a chlorine substituent and is named chloroethane.

questions.question: C6H5CH2Cl is classified as a ______.
questions.explanation: The chlorine-bearing carbon is directly adjacent to a benzene ring, so the compound is a benzylic halide.

questions.question: CH2=CHCl is classified as a ______.
questions.explanation: In CH2=CHCl, chlorine is directly attached to an sp2 carbon of a carbon-carbon double bond, making it a vinylic halide.

questions.question: C6H5Cl is classified as an ______.
questions.explanation: In C6H5Cl, chlorine is directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon, so the compound is an aryl halide.

questions.question: Due to the electronegativity difference between carbon and halogen, the C-X bond in a haloalkane is classified as ______.
questions.explanation: The halogen attracts the shared electron pair more strongly than carbon, making the C-X bond polar covalent.

questions.question: The reagent preferred for converting alcohols into alkyl chlorides because its by-products escape as gases is ______.
questions.explanation: Thionyl chloride, SOCl2, produces gaseous SO2 and HCl as by-products, allowing the alkyl chloride to be obtained in a relatively pure form.

questions.question: The named reaction used to prepare alkyl fluorides from alkyl chlorides or bromides is the ______.
questions.explanation: The Swarts reaction prepares alkyl fluorides by replacing chlorine or bromine in an alkyl halide with fluorine.

questions.question: The conversion of an alkyl chloride or bromide into an alkyl iodide using NaI in dry acetone is called the ______.
questions.explanation: The Finkelstein reaction uses NaI in dry acetone to convert alkyl chlorides or bromides into alkyl iodides.

questions.question: Conversion of a benzene diazonium salt into an aryl chloride or bromide using a Cu(I) salt is called the ______.
questions.explanation: The Sandmeyer reaction replaces the diazonium group with chlorine or bromine using the corresponding copper(I) salt.

questions.question: An electron-pair donor that attacks an electron-deficient centre is called a ______.
questions.explanation: A (weak)nucleophile donates an electron pair to an electron-deficient centre during a chemical reaction.

questions.question: A one-step nucleophilic substitution reaction mechanism involving backside attack is called ______.
questions.explanation: SN2 is a concerted one-step nucleophilic substitution mechanism involving backside attack.

questions.question: A pair of non-superimposable mirror-image stereoisomers are called ______.
questions.explanation: Enantiomers are stereoisomers that are non-superimposable mirror images of each other.

questions.question: The stereochemical change caused by backside attack in an SN2 reaction is called ______.
questions.explanation: Backside attack in an SN2 reaction causes inversion of configuration at the stereogenic carbon.

questions.question: Formation of a mixture (50%-50% enantiomers) through nucleophilic attack on both sides of a planar carbocation is called ______.
questions.explanation: Attack on both sides of a planar carbocation produces both configurations and leads to racemisation.

questions.question: The rule predicting the more substituted alkene as the major product of beta-elimination is the ______.
questions.explanation: Zaitsev's rule predicts that the more substituted alkene is generally the major product of beta-elimination.

questions.question: A compound containing an OH group directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon is called  ______.
questions.explanation: A phenol contains a hydroxyl group directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon.

questions.question: CH2=CHCH2OH is classified as an ______.
questions.explanation: The OH-bearing carbon is directly adjacent to a carbon-carbon double bond, so the compound is an allylic alcohol.

questions.question: The IUPAC name of CH3OCH3 is ______.
questions.explanation: CH3OCH3 is named methoxymethane according to the alkoxyalkane system of ether nomenclature.

questions.question: The C=O functional group present in aldehydes and ketones is called the ______ group.
questions.explanation: A carbon atom double-bonded to oxygen forms the carbonyl functional group.

questions.question: The simplest aldehyde used with a Grignard reagent to prepare a primary alcohol is ______.
questions.explanation: Methanal reacts with a Grignard reagent and, after hydrolysis, gives a primary alcohol.

questions.question: The carbonyl compound that gives propan-2-ol on reduction is ______.
questions.explanation: Reduction of propanone converts its carbonyl group into a hydroxyl group and forms propan-2-ol.

questions.question: Hydroboration-oxidation of an unsymmetrical alkene follows ______ orientation.
questions.explanation: Hydroboration-oxidation places the hydroxyl group on the less substituted carbon and follows anti-Markovnikov orientation.

questions.question: The intermolecular attraction mainly responsible for the high boiling points of alcohols and phenols is ______.
questions.explanation: Alcohols and phenols form intermolecular hydrogen bonds through their hydroxyl groups.

questions.question: The aromatic amine that gives phenol on hydrolysis of its diazonium salt is ______.
questions.explanation: Benzenamine, commonly known as aniline, is converted into a benzene diazonium salt, which on hydrolysis gives phenol.

questions.question: An alcohol whose OH-bearing carbon is bonded to three other carbon atoms is classified as a ______.
questions.explanation: In a tertiary alcohol, the carbon bearing the hydroxyl group is directly attached to three carbon atoms.

questions.question: The hybridisation of the oxygen atom in a simple alcohol is ______.
questions.explanation: The oxygen atom in a simple alcohol has two sigma bonds and two lone pairs and is treated as sp3 hybridised.

questions.question: An organomagnesium compound represented by RMgX is called______.
questions.explanation: Organomagnesium compounds represented by RMgX are called Grignard reagents.

questions.question: The reaction process in which an alcohol and a carboxylic acid form an ester is called ______.
questions.explanation: The reaction of an alcohol with a carboxylic acid to form an ester is called esterification.

questions.question: The introduction of a CH3CO group into an alcohol or phenol derivative is called ______.
questions.explanation: Acetylation is the introduction of an acetyl, CH3CO, group into a molecule.

questions.question: The common name of ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid is ______.
questions.explanation: Ortho-hydroxybenzoic acid is commonly known as salicylic acid.

questions.question: Why does the carbon atom bonded to halogen in a haloalkane behave as an electron-deficient centre?
options: Carbon attracts the bonding electron pair more strongly than halogen
The C-X bond is completely ionic
The more electronegative halogen pulls the shared electron pair towards itself
The halogen transfers a lone pair completely to carbon
Carbon forms a carbon-carbon double bond during bond polarisation
questions.explanation: Halogen is more electronegative than carbon and attracts the shared electron pair, giving carbon a partial positive charge.

questions.question: An alcohol gives an alkyl chloride and H3PO3 as a phosphorus-containing by-product. Which reagent was most likely used?
options: PCl5
SOCl2
NaCl
CuCl
PCl3
questions.explanation: PCl3 converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides and forms phosphorous acid, H3PO3, as a by-product.

questions.question: An alcohol is converted into an alkyl chloride with POCl3 and HCl formed as by-products. Which reagent accounts for these products?
options: PCl3
PCl5
SOCl2
NaCl
CuCl
questions.explanation: PCl5 converts an alcohol into an alkyl chloride while producing POCl3 and HCl as by-products.

questions.question: Why can an alkyl chloride prepared using SOCl2 often be obtained in a relatively pure form?
options: SOCl2 produces only insoluble solid by-products
SOCl2 prevents cleavage of the carbon-oxygen bond
SOCl2 converts impurities into Grignard reagents
The SO2 and HCl by-products escape as gases
The alkyl chloride precipitates from every solvent
questions.explanation: The SO2 and HCl by-products are gases and escape from the reaction mixture, leaving the alkyl chloride relatively pure.

questions.question: Why does the Finkelstein reaction proceed effectively when NaI is used in dry acetone?
options: NaCl or NaBr formed in the reaction precipitates from acetone
Acetone converts iodide into fluorine
NaI produces a stable carbocation in every substrate
Acetone supplies water for hydrolysis
The alkyl iodide forms an insoluble precipitate
questions.explanation: NaCl or NaBr is insoluble in dry acetone and precipitates, helping drive the halogen-exchange reaction forward.

questions.question: Why does KCN mainly give a nitrile whereas AgCN mainly gives an isocyanide with an alkyl halide?
options: KCN contains oxygen while AgCN contains nitrogen
AgCN converts the alkyl halide into an alkene first
KCN favours carbon-end attack, whereas AgCN favours nitrogen-end attack
KCN reacts only by elimination while AgCN reacts only by addition
Both reagents attack through the same atom but undergo different hydrolysis
questions.explanation: The cyanide group can attack through carbon or nitrogen. KCN favours carbon-end attack to form a nitrile, whereas AgCN favours nitrogen-end attack to form an isocyanide.

questions.question: Why does a tertiary haloalkane generally favour an SN1 mechanism over a primary haloalkane?
options: A tertiary haloalkane has no carbon-halogen bond
Primary haloalkanes always form aromatic intermediates
Tertiary haloalkanes prevent ionisation completely
Primary carbocations are more stable than tertiary carbocations
A tertiary haloalkane can form a relatively more stable carbocation
questions.explanation: The SN1 mechanism involves carbocation formation, and tertiary carbocations are relatively more stable than primary carbocations.

questions.question: Why does a methyl or primary haloalkane generally react more readily by an SN2 mechanism than a tertiary haloalkane?
options: It always forms the most stable carbocation
Lower steric hindrance allows easier backside attack
Its carbon-halogen bond is completely non-polar
It prevents nucleophiles from approaching the carbon atom
It undergoes racemisation before nucleophilic attack
questions.explanation: SN2 reactions require backside attack. Methyl and primary substrates have less steric hindrance around the reacting carbon.

questions.question: Why do polar protic solvents generally favour SN1 reactions?
options: They convert every nucleophile into a Grignard reagent
They prevent the carbon-halogen bond from breaking
They remove the halogen atom by beta-elimination only
They stabilise the ions formed during carbocation generation
They make tertiary carbocations less stable than primary carbocations
questions.explanation: Polar protic solvents stabilise the carbocation and leaving-group ions, facilitating the ionisation step of an SN1 reaction.

questions.question: Why can a polar aprotic solvent increase the effectiveness of an anionic nucleophile in an SN2 reaction?
options: It does not strongly solvate the anionic nucleophile through hydrogen bonding
It converts the nucleophile into a carbocation
It forces the reaction to proceed through a planar carbocation
It removes all lone pairs from the nucleophile
It converts the substrate into an aryl halide
questions.explanation: Polar aprotic solvents do not strongly hydrogen-bond to anionic nucleophiles, so the nucleophile remains more available for backside attack.

questions.question: Why does an SN1 reaction of an optically active haloalkane tend to produce a racemised mixture?
options: The nucleophile can attack only from the backside
The carbon-halogen bond becomes a double bond
The planar carbocation can be attacked from either side
The substrate undergoes beta-elimination before substitution
The nucleophile removes all four groups from the chiral carbon
questions.explanation: The planar carbocation intermediate can be attacked from either side, producing both configurations and causing racemisation.

questions.question: A substitution reaction occurs at a stereogenic carbon, but its original spatial arrangement is preserved in the product. Which stereochemical result has occurred?
options: Inversion
Racemisation
Beta-elimination
Enantiomerism
Retention
questions.explanation: Preservation of the original spatial arrangement around a stereogenic carbon is described as retention of configuration.

questions.question: Why does aqueous KOH generally favour substitution whereas alcoholic KOH generally favours elimination in haloalkanes?
options: Aqueous KOH forms only carbocations in every haloalkane
The aqueous medium favours nucleophilic substitution, while the alcoholic medium favours removal of beta-hydrogen
Alcoholic KOH converts every haloalkane into a Grignard reagent
Aqueous KOH prevents nucleophiles from approaching carbon
Both media favour exactly the same reaction pathway
questions.explanation: Aqueous KOH generally favours nucleophilic substitution, whereas alcoholic KOH favours beta-elimination involving removal of a beta-hydrogen.

questions.question: In beta-elimination of a haloalkane, why must the hydrogen removed be present on a carbon adjacent to the carbon bearing the halogen?
options: The hydrogen must first form a Grignard reagent
Only an aromatic hydrogen can be removed
The halogen must migrate to the beta-carbon
Removal from adjacent carbons allows formation of a carbon-carbon double bond
The hydrogen and halogen must be attached to the same atom
questions.explanation: Removal of hydrogen from the beta-carbon and halogen from the adjacent alpha-carbon allows a double bond to form between those two carbons.

questions.question: When 2-bromobutane undergoes beta-elimination, why is but-2-ene generally the major alkene?
options: But-2-ene is the more substituted alkene predicted as major by Zaitsev's rule
But-2-ene contains no carbon-carbon double bond
But-1-ene cannot be formed from 2-bromobutane
The bromine atom becomes part of but-2-ene
Elimination always forms the alkene with the terminal double bond
questions.explanation: According to Zaitsev's rule, the more substituted alkene is generally the major product, so but-2-ene predominates.

questions.question: Why are Grignard reagents highly reactive towards electron-deficient centres?
options: The C-Mg bond is completely non-polar
Magnesium removes all electrons from carbon
The polar C-Mg bond gives carbon carbanion-like character
The carbon atom carries a complete positive charge
Grignard reagents contain a carbon-carbon triple bond
questions.explanation: The strongly polar C-Mg bond gives the carbon atom substantial negative, carbanion-like character, making it highly reactive towards electron-deficient centres.

questions.question: Why is dry ether used during the preparation and handling of a Grignard reagent?
options: Ether supplies hydrogen ions to the reagent
Ether converts magnesium into sodium
Ether oxidises the carbon-magnesium bond
Ether produces water required for the reaction
Ether oxygen coordinates with magnesium and helps stabilise the reagent
questions.explanation: The lone pairs on ether oxygen coordinate with magnesium and help stabilise the Grignard reagent.

questions.question: Why must water be strictly excluded while using a Grignard reagent?
options: Water converts the reagent into an alkyl fluoride
Water protonates the reactive carbon and destroys the Grignard reagent
Water converts magnesium into sodium
Water changes the reagent into an aryl halide
Water makes the C-Mg bond completely non-polar
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents react rapidly with water. Water protonates the reactive carbon centre and destroys the reagent.

questions.question: Why are haloarenes generally less reactive than haloalkanes towards nucleophilic substitution?
options: Haloarenes contain no carbon-halogen bond
The aromatic carbon is always negatively charged
Halogens have no lone pairs in haloarenes
Resonance gives the carbon-halogen bond partial double-bond character
Haloarenes immediately form Grignard reagents in water
questions.explanation: Resonance gives the carbon-halogen bond in haloarenes partial double-bond character, making the bond shorter and stronger.

questions.question: Why are harsh conditions required to convert chlorobenzene into phenol using aqueous NaOH?
options: The carbon-chlorine bond in chlorobenzene is strengthened by resonance and requires about 623 K and 300 atm
Chlorobenzene is an ionic solid that must first be melted
NaOH reacts only below room temperature
Phenol is formed only in dry ether
Chlorobenzene must first be converted into an alkyl halide
questions.explanation: The carbon-chlorine bond in chlorobenzene has partial double-bond character due to resonance. NCERT describes heating with aqueous NaOH at 623 K and 300 atmospheres before acidification.

questions.question: Why does a nitro group at the ortho or para position increase the reactivity of a haloarene towards nucleophilic substitution?
options: It converts the carbon-halogen bond into a non-polar bond
It removes the halogen before nucleophilic attack
It helps stabilise the negatively charged intermediate by electron withdrawal
It converts the nucleophile into an electrophile
It prevents the nucleophile from approaching the aromatic ring
questions.explanation: An ortho or para nitro group withdraws electron density and stabilises the negatively charged intermediate formed during nucleophilic substitution.

questions.question: Why are halogens deactivating but ortho-para directing during electrophilic substitution of haloarenes?
options: They activate the ring only through the inductive effect
They remove aromaticity permanently
They direct all electrophiles exclusively to the meta position
They have no electronic effect on the aromatic ring
Their inductive effect deactivates the ring, while lone-pair resonance directs substitution to ortho and para positions
questions.explanation: Halogens withdraw electron density through the inductive effect but donate lone-pair electron density by resonance, making them deactivating yet ortho-para directing.

questions.question: When two molecules of CH3Br react with sodium in dry ether, why is ethane formed in the Wurtz reaction?
options: Each methyl group is converted into a hydroxyl group
Two methyl carbon fragments become coupled to form a new carbon-carbon bond
Bromine atoms combine to form the carbon skeleton
Sodium converts methane directly into ethane
The reaction introduces a carbon-carbon double bond
questions.explanation: The Wurtz reaction couples two alkyl fragments. Coupling two methyl groups forms a new carbon-carbon bond and produces ethane.

questions.question: Why are haloalkanes generally only slightly soluble in water despite having a polar carbon-halogen bond?
options: Haloalkanes form stronger hydrogen bonds than water
All haloalkanes are ionic solids
Water immediately converts haloalkanes into alkenes
Haloalkane-water attractions do not compensate sufficiently for breaking hydrogen bonds between water molecules
The carbon-halogen bond prevents all intermolecular attraction
questions.explanation: The energy released by interactions between haloalkanes and water is insufficient to compensate for the energy needed to break hydrogen bonds between water molecules.

questions.question: Why can DDT become increasingly concentrated at higher trophic levels of a food chain?
options: Its persistence and poor biodegradability allow it to accumulate and undergo biomagnification
It is immediately decomposed into harmless substances in organisms
It is highly soluble in water and is removed instantly
It is produced naturally by every organism in a food chain
Its concentration decreases each time one organism consumes another
questions.explanation: DDT is persistent and poorly biodegradable, so it accumulates in organisms and its concentration can increase at successive trophic levels.

questions.question: Why is the oxygen atom in a simple alcohol treated as sp3 hybridised?
options: It has one sigma bond and three lone pairs
It has one double bond and one lone pair
It has two sigma bonds and two lone pairs
It has three pi bonds and no lone pair
It has two double bonds and two lone pairs
questions.explanation: Alcohol oxygen has two sigma bonds and two lone pairs, giving four electron regions and an sp3 hybridised description.

questions.question: Two compounds both contain an OH group. Why is C6H5OH classified as a phenol rather than an alcohol?
options: Its oxygen atom contains no lone pairs
Its OH group is attached to a saturated primary carbon
It contains an ether linkage
Its OH group is attached to a carbonyl carbon
Its OH group is directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon
questions.explanation: C6H5OH is classified as a phenol because the hydroxyl group is directly attached to an aromatic ring carbon.

questions.question: Why is CH3OCH2CH3 named methoxyethane rather than ethoxymethane in IUPAC nomenclature?
options: The oxygen atom must always be included in the parent chain
The smaller methyl group is named as the methoxy substituent and ethane is selected as the parent
The larger group must always be named as an alkoxy substituent
Ether compounds are named as alcohols
The molecule contains a carbonyl group
questions.explanation: In alkoxyalkane nomenclature, the larger carbon chain is selected as the parent and the smaller group with oxygen is named as the alkoxy substituent.

questions.question: Why is the carbon bearing the OH group assigned carbon number 1 in a simple cyclic alcohol containing one hydroxyl group?
options: The OH group is ignored during numbering
The ring must be numbered alphabetically
The largest substituent always receives carbon number 1
The principal hydroxyl functional group receives the lowest locant
Every cyclic compound begins numbering from an unsubstituted carbon
questions.explanation: The hydroxyl group is the principal functional group in the cyclic alcohol and its carbon is assigned the lowest locant, carbon 1.

questions.question: Why does acid-catalysed hydration of propene mainly produce propan-2-ol rather than propan-1-ol?
options: The addition follows Markovnikov orientation, placing OH on the more substituted carbon
Water removes the terminal carbon atom of propene
The reaction follows anti-Markovnikov orientation
Propene is first converted into benzene
The OH group must always attach to carbon number 1
questions.explanation: Acid-catalysed hydration follows Markovnikov orientation, so the hydroxyl group becomes attached to the more substituted carbon and propan-2-ol is the major product.

questions.question: Why does hydroboration-oxidation of propene mainly produce propan-1-ol?
options: The OH group is removed from the final product
The reaction always produces a phenol
The reaction converts the double bond into a carbonyl group
The OH group is placed on the more substituted carbon
The reaction follows anti-Markovnikov orientation and places OH on the less substituted carbon
questions.explanation: Hydroboration-oxidation follows anti-Markovnikov orientation, placing the hydroxyl group on the less substituted carbon and producing propan-1-ol.

questions.question: Why does reduction of an aldehyde generally produce a primary alcohol?
options: The carbonyl carbon is bonded to three carbon groups
The aldehyde carbonyl carbon has a hydrogen and is converted into an OH-bearing carbon characteristic of a primary alcohol
The aldehyde loses its carbonyl carbon during reduction
Reduction directly attaches the OH group to an aromatic ring
Every aldehyde first becomes a ketone
questions.explanation: The aldehyde carbonyl carbon already bears a hydrogen. Reduction converts the carbonyl group into an alcohol group, generally producing a primary alcohol.

questions.question: Why does reduction of a ketone generally produce a secondary alcohol?
options: A ketone contains no carbonyl group
Reduction removes one carbon group from the ketone
The ketone carbonyl carbon is bonded to two carbon groups, which remain attached after reduction
The OH group becomes directly attached to an aromatic ring
Ketones are converted into primary carbocations before reduction
questions.explanation: The carbonyl carbon of a ketone is bonded to two carbon groups. These remain attached when the carbonyl group is reduced, giving a secondary alcohol.

questions.question: Why does reaction of a Grignard reagent with methanal followed by hydrolysis produce a primary alcohol?
options: Methanal already contains two alkyl groups
The Grignard reagent removes the carbonyl carbon
Hydrolysis converts the product into a phenol
After carbon-carbon bond formation, the OH-bearing carbon is attached to only one carbon group
Methanal prevents carbon-carbon bond formation
questions.explanation: After addition of a Grignard reagent to methanal and hydrolysis, the hydroxyl-bearing carbon is attached to only one carbon group, so a primary alcohol is formed.

questions.question: A Grignard reagent reacts with an aldehyde other than methanal and the product is hydrolysed. Why is a secondary alcohol generally formed?
options: The OH-bearing carbon in the product is attached to two carbon groups
The aldehyde loses all of its hydrogen atoms
The Grignard reagent converts the aldehyde into an ether
The carbonyl carbon is removed during hydrolysis
A benzene ring is formed before the alcohol
questions.explanation: An aldehyde other than methanal already has one carbon group attached to the carbonyl carbon. Grignard addition introduces another, so the final OH-bearing carbon is attached to two carbon groups.

questions.question: Why does reaction of a Grignard reagent with a ketone followed by hydrolysis generally produce a tertiary alcohol?
options: The ketone loses both carbon groups during the reaction
The Grignard reagent removes the oxygen atom completely
The ketone carbonyl carbon has two carbon groups and Grignard addition introduces a third carbon group
Hydrolysis converts every ketone into a phenol
The reaction prevents formation of a carbon-carbon bond
questions.explanation: A ketone carbonyl carbon already has two carbon groups. Grignard addition introduces a third carbon group, and hydrolysis gives a tertiary alcohol.

questions.question: During preparation of phenol from chlorobenzene using aqueous NaOH under harsh conditions, why is acidification required after formation of sodium phenoxide?
options: Acidification converts phenol into chlorobenzene
Acidification forms a Grignard reagent
Acidification removes the aromatic ring
Acidification converts sodium phenoxide into an ether
Acidification protonates the phenoxide species to give phenol
questions.explanation: The reaction with aqueous NaOH first forms sodium phenoxide. Acidification protonates the phenoxide species and produces phenol.

questions.question: In the cumene process, why is acetone obtained along with phenol?
options: Phenol is oxidised directly into acetone
Acidic decomposition of cumene hydroperoxide produces both phenol and acetone
Acetone is used only as an unreacted solvent
Benzene is reduced directly to acetone
Phenol reacts with water to form acetone
questions.explanation: Cumene is converted into cumene hydroperoxide, whose acidic decomposition produces phenol and acetone.

questions.question: Why do alcohols generally have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons of comparable molecular mass?
options: Alcohols are always ionic
Alcohols contain carbon-carbon triple bonds
Alcohol molecules form intermolecular hydrogen bonds
Hydrocarbons form stronger hydrogen bonds than alcohols
Alcohols have no intermolecular forces other than covalent bonds
questions.explanation: Intermolecular hydrogen bonding between alcohol molecules requires additional energy to overcome, resulting in relatively high boiling points.

questions.question: Why does the solubility of alcohols in water generally decrease as the alkyl chain becomes longer?
options: The OH group loses its oxygen atom
Longer alcohols become ionic compounds
Hydrogen bonding with water becomes chemically impossible
The increasing hydrophobic alkyl portion has a greater influence on the molecule
The carbon chain converts the alcohol into a phenol
questions.explanation: Although the hydroxyl group can hydrogen-bond with water, the growing hydrophobic alkyl portion increasingly reduces water solubility.

questions.question: When an alcohol reacts with sodium metal, why is hydrogen gas evolved?
options: The O-H bond is cleaved and the acidic hydrogen is replaced by sodium
The carbon-carbon bond breaks to release hydrogen
Sodium converts oxygen directly into hydrogen
The alcohol undergoes beta-elimination
The alkyl group is converted into a Grignard reagent
questions.explanation: Reaction with sodium involves cleavage of the O-H bond. The hydroxyl hydrogen is replaced by sodium, and hydrogen gas is evolved.

questions.question: Why is phenol more acidic than a typical alcohol?
options: Phenol contains an ionic O-H bond
Alcohols have no oxygen atom
Phenol releases a carbon atom when it loses hydrogen
The phenoxide species has no negative charge
The phenoxide species is stabilised by resonance, whereas an alkoxide lacks comparable resonance stabilisation
questions.explanation: The negative charge in the phenoxide species is delocalised by resonance over the aromatic ring, making it more stable than a typical alkoxide.

questions.question: Why does an electron-withdrawing substituent generally increase the acidic character of phenol?
options: It prevents cleavage of the O-H bond
It helps stabilise the negatively charged phenoxide species
It converts phenol into an alcohol
It removes the oxygen atom from phenol
It increases electron density on the phenoxide species
questions.explanation: An electron-withdrawing group helps disperse or reduce the effect of negative charge in the phenoxide species, increasing its stability and favouring proton loss.

questions.question: Why can an alcohol act as a nucleophile in a chemical reaction?
options: The carbon atom always carries a complete negative charge
The O-H bond is completely ionic
The oxygen atom has lone pairs that can be donated to an electron-deficient centre
The oxygen atom has no non-bonding electrons
The alkyl group always forms a carbocation first
questions.explanation: The oxygen atom of an alcohol possesses lone pairs and can donate an electron pair to an electron-deficient centre, allowing the alcohol to act as a nucleophile.

questions.question: Why does protonation of the OH group make an alcohol more susceptible to nucleophilic substitution at carbon?
options: Protonation converts carbon into a nucleophile
Protonation removes the oxygen atom instantly
The alkyl group becomes aromatic
Protonation allows water to act as a better leaving group
The carbon-oxygen bond becomes a carbon-carbon bond
questions.explanation: Protonation converts the poor leaving OH group into a form that can leave as water, making substitution at carbon more favourable.

questions.question: Why can an alcohol show basic behaviour in the presence of an acid?
options: The oxygen lone pair can accept a proton
The alcohol donates its carbon skeleton to the acid
The oxygen atom has a complete positive charge before reaction
The alcohol must first undergo beta-elimination
The alkyl group accepts a halogen atom instead of a proton
questions.explanation: The oxygen atom has lone pairs and can accept a proton from an acid, allowing an alcohol to behave as a base.

questions.question: Why does heating an alcohol with a carboxylic acid in the presence of an acid catalyst lead to ester formation?
options: The alcohol is converted into a haloalkane first
The carboxylic acid is converted into a Grignard reagent
The reaction combines the alcohol and carboxylic acid components with elimination of water
The aromatic ring of the acid is destroyed
The alcohol loses its entire alkyl group
questions.explanation: In esterification, an alcohol and a carboxylic acid react under acid catalysis to form an ester with elimination of water.

questions.question: Why are acetyl chloride or acetic anhydride used in acetylation of an alcohol or phenol?
options: They introduce a halogen directly onto the aromatic ring
They remove the hydroxyl oxygen
They convert the compound into a Grignard reagent
They cause only beta-elimination
They provide an acetyl group that becomes attached through oxygen
questions.explanation: Acetyl chloride and acetic anhydride act as acetylating reagents and introduce an acetyl group through the oxygen of an alcohol or phenol.

questions.question: Why is sodium phenoxide used instead of phenol directly in the Kolbe reaction for the preparation of salicylic acid?
options: Sodium phenoxide converts carbon dioxide into carbon monoxide
The phenoxide group increases electron density in the aromatic ring and facilitates reaction with carbon dioxide
Phenol cannot contain an aromatic ring
Sodium phenoxide prevents carboxylation of the aromatic ring
The sodium atom becomes part of the final salicylic acid molecule
questions.explanation: The phenoxide group increases electron density in the aromatic ring, making it more reactive towards carbon dioxide during the Kolbe reaction. Acidification subsequently gives salicylic acid.

questions.question: Why does phenol react with aqueous NaOH whereas a typical alcohol generally does not?
options: Alcohols contain no O-H bond
Phenol is a stronger base than NaOH
Alcohols are always insoluble in aqueous NaOH
Phenol is sufficiently more acidic because its conjugate species is resonance stabilised
NaOH converts phenol directly into a Grignard reagent
questions.explanation: Phenol is more acidic than a typical alcohol because the phenoxide species is resonance stabilised, allowing phenol to react with aqueous NaOH.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of reactivity of alkyl halides towards an SN1 reaction?
questions.explanation: SN1 reactivity follows carbocation stability. Tertiary carbocations are most stable, followed by secondary and primary carbocations.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of reactivity of alkyl halides towards an SN2 reaction?
questions.explanation: SN2 reactivity decreases with increasing steric hindrance. Methyl halides react fastest, followed by primary, secondary and tertiary halides.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of carbon-halogen bond strength in methyl halides?
options: C-I > C-Br > C-Cl > C-F
C-Br > C-Cl > C-F > C-I
C-Cl > C-F > C-Br > C-I
C-F > C-Cl > C-Br > C-I
C-F > C-Br > C-Cl > C-I
questions.explanation: Carbon-halogen bond strength decreases as the size of the halogen increases: C-F > C-Cl > C-Br > C-I.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of reactivity of methyl halides towards nucleophilic substitution?
options: CH3I > CH3Br > CH3Cl > CH3F
CH3F > CH3Cl > CH3Br > CH3I
CH3Cl > CH3Br > CH3I > CH3F
CH3Br > CH3I > CH3Cl > CH3F
CH3I > CH3Cl > CH3Br > CH3F
questions.explanation: The C-I bond is weakest and breaks most readily, while the C-F bond is strongest. Therefore, reactivity follows CH3I > CH3Br > CH3Cl > CH3F.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of boiling points of the following chlorinated methanes?
options: CH3Cl > CH2Cl2 > CHCl3 > CCl4
CHCl3 > CCl4 > CH2Cl2 > CH3Cl
CCl4 > CHCl3 > CH2Cl2 > CH3Cl
CH2Cl2 > CH3Cl > CHCl3 > CCl4
CCl4 > CH2Cl2 > CHCl3 > CH3Cl
questions.explanation: Boiling point generally increases with molecular mass and strength of dispersion forces. The order is CCl4 > CHCl3 > CH2Cl2 > CH3Cl.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of acidity?
options: Ethanol > water > phenol
Water > phenol > ethanol
Phenol > ethanol > water
Ethanol > phenol > water
Phenol > water > ethanol
questions.explanation: Phenol is more acidic because the phenoxide ion is resonance stabilised. Water is slightly more acidic than ethanol because the ethyl group destabilises the ethoxide ion by its electron-releasing effect.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of acidity of substituted phenols?
options: p-Cresol > phenol > p-nitrophenol
p-Nitrophenol > phenol > p-cresol
Phenol > p-nitrophenol > p-cresol
p-Nitrophenol > p-cresol > phenol
Phenol > p-cresol > p-nitrophenol
questions.explanation: The nitro group increases acidity by stabilising the phenoxide ion, whereas the methyl group decreases acidity through its electron-releasing effect.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of boiling points?
options: Propane > methoxyethane > propan-1-ol
Methoxyethane > propan-1-ol > propane
Propan-1-ol > methoxyethane > propane
Propan-1-ol > propane > methoxyethane
Methoxyethane > propane > propan-1-ol
questions.explanation: Propan-1-ol forms intermolecular hydrogen bonds and has the highest boiling point. Ether has stronger intermolecular attractions than the comparable hydrocarbon.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of solubility of the following alcohols in water?
options: Butan-1-ol > propan-1-ol > ethanol > methanol
Methanol > ethanol > propan-1-ol > butan-1-ol
Ethanol > methanol > butan-1-ol > propan-1-ol
Propan-1-ol > butan-1-ol > ethanol > methanol
Methanol > propan-1-ol > ethanol > butan-1-ol
questions.explanation: Water solubility of alcohols decreases as the hydrophobic alkyl chain becomes longer.

questions.question: Which is the correct decreasing order of boiling points of isomeric butyl alcohols?
options: tert-Butyl alcohol > sec-butyl alcohol > isobutyl alcohol > n-butyl alcohol
n-Butyl alcohol > isobutyl alcohol > sec-butyl alcohol > tert-butyl alcohol
sec-Butyl alcohol > n-butyl alcohol > tert-butyl alcohol > isobutyl alcohol
isobutyl alcohol > n-butyl alcohol > tert-butyl alcohol > sec-butyl alcohol
n-Butyl alcohol > sec-butyl alcohol > isobutyl alcohol > tert-butyl alcohol
questions.explanation: Among isomeric alcohols, increased branching lowers boiling point by reducing surface area and weakening dispersion forces. Therefore, the order is n-butyl alcohol > isobutyl alcohol > sec-butyl alcohol > tert-butyl alcohol.
